children.text: A sonic history
"The snares suggest connections to a long history of
pistas

and mixtaps which preceded the breakthrough pop smash and which remain as audible, palpable, if subtle remnants of an unbridled, underground, sample-based past in a genre that has since embraced slick synthesizers and commercial channels" (Marshall, 2009 p. 21).
Origin Narrative

Reggaetón is often described as a Puerto Rican innovation with origins in the underground scene of San Juan during the 1990s. This narrative frames reggaetón as the product of artistic codemixing emerging from the simultaneous interplay of Afro-Caribbean musical influences resonating in Jamaica, Panama, Puerto Rico, and, importantly, New York City.

"In New York, Panamanians, Jamaicans, Puerto Ricans, Dominicans, and African Americans all lived in proximity and were often racialized together. Therefore, genres like reggae and hip-hop resonanted powerfully as "Black musics" which, for all lines still drawn between groups, could embody and express new articulations of community" (Marshall, 2009 p. 33).

"Dancehall and hip-hop have tended to travel together, heard outside their principal sites of production as two sides of the same coin" (Marshall, 2009 p. 25). Dancehall made its way to San Juan not from Kingston, but from New York.
This direct link between Jamaica and Panama and the embrace of reggae produced a different ecology that than in Puerto Rico, to where the genre arrived indirectly as mediated by New York and hip-hop.
Dancehall Reggae & The Dem Bow Beat

The predominant rhythmic orientation of reggaetón is derived directly from dancehall reggae. While there is also influence from roots reggae, dancehall's textures, tempos, rapid-fire flow, minimalist focus on 3+3+2 cross rhythms demarcated by heavy, synthesized drums, and local concerns with crime, drugs, violence, sex, poverty, and corruption have had a stronger stylistic influence. Much of dancehall's uptake in Panama and Puerto Rico was related specifically to its counter-cultural connotations and close relationship with rap/hip-hop, in particular its capacity to circulate social semiotics of modernity, urbanity, and Blackness as political subjectivity. One of the most enduring features is the following
riddim
, a 4/4 kick drum pulse with syncopated snares popularized in Shabba Ranks' song "Dem Bow" (1990).

children.caption: Skeleton of the Dem Bow Riddim from Marshall (2009)

children.text: With the growth and influence of New York's Jamaican community in the 1980s, by the 1990s dancehall had worked its way into the sets of hip-hop DJs and became a culturally charged feature of the New York soundscape. These riddims, such as the examples below, would later be incorporated into the underground recordings that laid the musical foundations for reggaetón.
Latin Hip-Hop &
Rap en Español
While dancehall reggae gave the proto-reggaetón of the early 1990s its musical structure, the artists of this period —
raperos
or
rapeadores
not yet
reggaetoneros

— drew almost equally on reggae and hip-hop influences. In fact, these artists aligned closely with gangster rap and the "Latin rap" that diverged linguistically from this sonic template. Puerto Rico's Vico C stands as a foundational figure here, as the artist who first demonstrated that one could rap entirely and compellingly in Spanish (aside from the occasional insertion of hip-hop slang lyrics). In fact, many early reggaetón artists who began as aspiring rappers describe reggaetón as "essentially hip-hop, but with a flavor more compatible to the Caribbean", where the focus is more on establishing a baile-centric beat rather than the lyrics, which comes directly from the genre's dancehall baseline (Chosen Few Documentary).
"I sing hip-hop on top of a reggaetón beat. I don't know how to write it any other way" (Tego Calderón, Marshall 2009 p. 27)
Afro-Panamanian
Reggae en Español

Prior to the global rise of reggae in the 1970s, Afro-Panamanian popular music was heavily based around the calypso performances of local favorites like Lord Cobra and Lord Panama. By the late 1970s, roots reggae had largely supplanted calypso, and from here, artists like El General, Nando Boom, and Chicho Man became early proponents of Panamanian reggae by performing some of the first Spanish-language reggae tracks. Since the 1980s, dancehall has dominated the scene through the consistent production of Spanish-langauge cover versions of contemporary dancehall hits replayed over the original Jamaican riddims. In this sense, Panamanian reggae diverges from Puerto Rican reggaetón in that the former is an emulation of dancehall and the latter a derivation.
These remakes played a key role in shaping the dancehall scene in Puerto Rico, in particular the Columbia Records production
Dancehall Reggaespañol

(1991, prod. Karl Miller) that provided a template for pairing Spanish-language lyrics with Jamaican riddims. This product not only attests close engagement with contemporary reggae, but also bears witness to the crucial role New York played in shaping reggaetón production.
Meren-Rap
Following
Dancehall Reggaespañol
, the 8-track EP
Meren-Rap

(prod. Jorge Oquendo) brought together rising Puerto Rican rappers with established merengue musicians in a calculated attempt to develop a Laitn pop/rap hybrid that could appeal to a wider audience of rap, reggae, merengue, and pop fans. Most of these tracks were produced by Vico C. The song "Te Ves Buena Remix," —a sample-based, hip-hop-inflected reggae style track— is perhaps the closest track on this project to sound like the productions soon to emerge from the parallel, grassroots development of rap-reggae fusions in San Juan.

This studio experiment is best understood as playing a paradoxically important yet parginal role in the development of reggaetón. The album did make a splash on the Island, but without grassroots support it was quickly supplanted by a similar sort of fusion which, instead of Latin or "tropical" signifiers, tended to foreground the Black, urban, transnational sounds of hip-hop and reggae.
Melaza, Dembow, y el Underground
Meren-Rap
and
Dancehall Reggaespañol
found their way to San Juan via Jorge Oquendo and DJ Negro, the latter who began operating The Noise nightclub in 1990. Within this setting,
DJ Playero, DJ Negro, DJ Nelson put the tracks together
Ivy Queen
Daddy Yankee
Baby Rasta and Gringo propel their verses by intoning one of the many familiar melodic contours that Jamaican dancehall DJs have endlessly reworked since the early to mid-1980s
The different names for the genre all had different was of connecting with its Black diasporic roots.
- Calling it melaza (molasses, race as sugar production) directly connects it to cultural politics of Blackness.
- Calling it underground or dembow, or rap and reggae, signaled artistic alignment and a localization of products putatively non-Puerto Rican (rap & reggae).
- Underground calls attention to its marginalized (and proud) status vis-a-vis mainstream Puerto Rican economy, culture, and society.

Even though these terms are derived from hip-hop and reggae, they took on new meaning in Puerto Rico to describe "una fusión tan intensa de rap con reggae que no puede ser clasificada como una cosa o la otra" (Rivera, 1995 fn 50)

Moreover, the term underground referred specifically to how underground recordings literally circulated outside of formal commercial channels, dubbed from cassette to cassette after an initial, small run of master tapes until about 1994. This was in part due to how the music had a reputation as the obscene music of the underclass (e.g., clashes with the National Center on Sexual Exploitation, NCOSE, formerly Morality in Media kept them out of mainstream channels ).
It wasn't until 1999 when DJ Nelson and DJ Coyote managed to get San Juan's mix 107.7 fm to begin a 24/7 reggaetón format

The dembow name refers to the beat from Shabba Rank's production, though it has been abstracted away from its original into a particular rhythmic pattern and a set of specific drum timbres. This became an important signifier that artists could combine with other elements to create distinctiveness within a system of resonant sonic signifiers.

content.caption: Basic Elements of Puerto Rico's localized Dem Bow

children.text: DJ Negro & DJ Playero embodied the process of localizing the foreign-but-familiar through their influential mixtape sets that aided the creation of reggaetón's current style by building off reggae's "riddim method" and hip-hop's sample-based collages. The degree of intertextuality on such recordings is remarkable and charged with significance.

Underground producers employ a primarily sample-based approach according to whatever source seems appropriate, rearticulating both reggae and hip-hop's central practices of reuse and allusion. Marshall (p. 45) describes this as taking reggae's riddim method and hip-hop omnivorous sampling to an unprecedented extreme.
Reggaetón Latino
Luney Tunes
alternates the snares every four measures to create a subtle, stylistically grounded sense of movement against the otherwise repetitive structures of the track
